SEARCH
Module DSA
Package com.dsa.pattern

Class FastAndSlowPointer

java.lang.Object
com.dsa.pattern.FastAndSlowPointer
public class FastAndSlowPointer extends Object
Demonstrates the Fast and Slow Pointer Pattern (also called Floyd’s Tortoise and Hare).

This pattern uses two pointers moving at different speeds through a data structure, commonly used for cycle detection, finding middle elements, or detecting duplicates.

Key Idea

  • Maintain a slow pointer that moves one step at a time
  • Maintain a fast pointer that moves two steps at a time
  • If a cycle exists, the fast pointer will eventually meet the slow pointer
  • Can also be used to find the middle of a linked list efficiently

When to Use

  • Detect cycles in linked lists or sequences
  • Find middle element in a linked list
  • Check for repeated states in iterative sequences

Example Implementation (Cycle Detection in Linked List)


 package com.dsa.pattern;

 static class ListNode {
 	int val;
 	ListNode next;
 
 	ListNode(int val) {
 		this.val = val;
 	}
 }

 public class FastAndSlowPointerExample {

 	// Detect cycle in a linked list
 	public static boolean hasCycle(ListNode head) {
 		if (head == null)
 			return false;
 		ListNode slow = head, fast = head;
 		while (fast != null && fast.next != null) {
 			slow = slow.next;
 			fast = fast.next.next;
 			if (slow == fast)
 				return true; // cycle detected
 		}
 		return false;
 	}

 	// Find middle element of linked list
 	public static ListNode findMiddle(ListNode head) {
 		if (head == null)
 			return null;
 		ListNode slow = head, fast = head;
 		while (fast != null && fast.next != null) {
 			slow = slow.next;
 			fast = fast.next.next;
 		}
 		return slow;
 	}

 	public static void main(String[] args) {
 		ListNode head = new ListNode(1);
 		head.next = new ListNode(2);
 		head.next.next = new ListNode(3);
 		head.next.next.next = new ListNode(4);

 		// Create a cycle for testing
 		head.next.next.next.next = head.next;

 		System.out.println("Cycle detected? " + hasCycle(head));

 		// Remove cycle to find middle
 		head.next.next.next.next = null;
 		System.out.println("Middle node value: " + findMiddle(head).val);
 	}
 }

Complexity

  • Time: O(n) for traversing the list
  • Space: O(1) – constant extra space

Applications

  • Detecting cycles in linked lists
  • Finding middle elements efficiently
  • Floyd’s cycle-finding algorithm in sequences
